title: The Owner-Dependence Discount: What Buyers Price In Before Making You an Offer
linkedin:
Most owner-led businesses are worth more on paper than they receive at settlement.

Not because the financials are wrong. Because a buyer looked at how the business runs without the owner in the room, and they priced what they saw.

Owner dependence is one of the most common sources of valuation adjustment in SME sales, and one of the least visible from inside the business. The key clients call you. The significant decisions route through you. The institutional knowledge lives with you. None of that is a problem while you are in the seat. It becomes a problem when someone is calculating what they are actually buying.

Here is how buyers respond: they build the cost of filling that gap into the deal before the first offer is made. Sometimes it shows up as a lower multiple. More often it shows up as an earn-out, where part of the price is paid only if the business hits targets post-settlement, in a business the seller no longer controls.

The structural changes required take more time than most owners plan for. Client relationships need to be genuinely transitioned to other team members, not introduced when the business is clearly preparing for sale. Management depth needs to appear in the historical data, not just the current org chart. Buyers can tell the difference.

For businesses where a sale is a possibility in the next two to four years, EOFY is a useful moment to map where owner dependence sits and which changes need the most lead time.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

#BusinessValuation #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
One question sits behind almost every business sale negotiation, even if it is never asked directly: does this business run because of the owner, or does it run because of the systems and team the owner built?

Buyers answer that question through due diligence. And the answer shapes not just the number they offer, but how much of that number arrives unconditionally on settlement day.

With June 30 coming up and a full year of trading data about to become available, it is a good moment to ask yourself honestly: what would slow or stop in your business if you were genuinely unreachable for a month?

Curious what other Queensland and NSW business owners have found when they've asked themselves that question.

#BusinessOwners #AustralianBusiness
google:
For owner-operated businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, and New South Wales, one of the most common gaps between what an owner expects to receive in a sale and what a buyer actually offers comes down to owner dependence.

Buyers assess it directly during due diligence. They look at which clients call the owner personally, which decisions slow or stop without the owner's input, and how well the business is documented for the team to operate independently. Where the answer to those questions is "most things depend on the owner," the risk gets priced into the deal structure, sometimes as a lower multiple, more often as an earn-out where part of the price is contingent on post-settlement performance.

The structural changes that address owner dependence take 12 to 24 months to appear meaningfully in the record a buyer will examine. The businesses that achieve the strongest, cleanest sale terms are typically the ones that started this work two to three years before a planned exit.

With the end of the financial year twelve days away and a full year of trading data becoming available, now is a practical moment to take stock of where your business sits across the dimensions buyers will assess. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
